patterns.name: AWS Access Key Exposed in Logs

solutions.title: Immediately invalidate the exposed key
solutions.command_template:
# Deactivate via AWS CLI:
aws iam update-access-key \
  --access-key-id <key-id> \
  --status Inactive \
  --user-name <iam-user>

# Or delete it entirely:
aws iam delete-access-key \
  --access-key-id <key-id> \
  --user-name <iam-user>
solutions.explanation: An exposed AWS access key must be rotated immediately. Even if the key was only briefly visible in logs, assume it was captured by automated scanners. Deactivate first (reversible), then investigate, then delete.

solutions.title: Check CloudTrail for unauthorized use of the key
solutions.command_template:
# Look for API calls using the exposed key in the last 24h:
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
  --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=AccessKeyId,AttributeValue=<key-id> \
  --start-time $(date -d '24 hours ago' +%s)

# Check for unusual regions or services
solutions.explanation: After deactivating the key, audit CloudTrail to determine if and how it was used. Check for IAM changes, S3 data access, EC2 launches, and cross-region activity.

solutions.title: Remove the key from source and add to .gitignore / pre-commit hooks
solutions.command_template:
# Remove from git history using git-filter-repo:
pip install git-filter-repo
git filter-repo --path .env --invert-paths

# Add pre-commit secret scanner:
# .pre-commit-config.yaml:
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks
    rev: v8.18.0
    hooks:
      - id: gitleaks
solutions.explanation: After remediation, prevent recurrence by adding secret scanning to pre-commit hooks (gitleaks, detect-secrets) and enabling GitHub Advanced Security or GitGuardian for repository-level scanning.

solutions.title: Immediately revoke the exposed GitHub token
solutions.command_template:
# Via GitHub UI:
# Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Revoke

# Via API:
curl -X DELETE \
  -H "Authorization: token <your-admin-token>" \
  https://api.github.com/applications/<client_id>/token \
  -d '{"access_token":"<exposed-token>"}'
solutions.explanation: GitHub PATs grant broad repository and organization access. Revoke immediately via the GitHub UI or API. GitHub's Secret Scanning may auto-revoke fine-grained PATs for select patterns.

solutions.title: Audit GitHub audit log for token usage
solutions.command_template:
# GitHub Enterprise — query audit log via API:
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <admin-token>" \
  "https://api.github.com/enterprises/<enterprise>/audit-log?phrase=token_id:<token>&per_page=100"
solutions.explanation: Check the GitHub audit log for repository clones, pushes, workflow triggers, and organization changes performed with the exposed token.

solutions.title: Replace with GitHub Actions OIDC or fine-grained PAT stored as secret
solutions.command_template:
# In GitHub Actions — use built-in GITHUB_TOKEN (already scoped):
- name: Checkout
  uses: actions/checkout@v4
  with:
    token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

# For cross-repo access — use a fine-grained PAT stored as a secret:
# Repository Settings → Secrets → Actions → New repository secret
solutions.explanation: Never hardcode GitHub tokens. Use the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` for same-repo operations, or a fine-grained PAT scoped to minimum permissions stored in GitHub Secrets.

patterns.name: Private Key Exposed in Logs or Repository

solutions.title: Immediately revoke and regenerate the private key
solutions.explanation: Private keys (SSH, TLS, code signing) that appear in logs or source code must be considered compromised. Revoke the associated certificate or public key from all services (GitHub SSH keys, TLS certificate provider, cloud providers), then generate a new key pair.

solutions.title: Store keys in a secrets manager, not in code or environment output
solutions.command_template:
# AWS Secrets Manager:
aws secretsmanager create-secret \
  --name prod/ssh-deploy-key \
  --secret-string file://deploy_key

# GitHub Actions — store as encrypted secret:
# Repository Settings → Secrets → Actions → New secret
# Then reference: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEY }}

# HashiCorp Vault:
vault kv put secret/deploy-key private="$(cat deploy_key)"
solutions.explanation: Private keys should be stored in a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GitHub Actions secrets) with audit logging, rotation support, and least-privilege access controls.

solutions.title: Remove from git history with git-filter-repo
solutions.command_template:
pip install git-filter-repo
git filter-repo --path private_key.pem --invert-paths --force
git push --force-with-lease
solutions.explanation: If the key was committed to git, removing it from HEAD is not enough — it remains in history. Use git-filter-repo to rewrite history, then force-push. Notify all collaborators to re-clone.

patterns.name: Generic API Key or Token Detected in Logs

solutions.title: Identify and rotate the exposed credential immediately
solutions.explanation: Identify which service the key belongs to from context (Stripe, Twilio, SendGrid, OpenAI, etc.) and rotate it immediately via that service's dashboard. Check access logs for unauthorized usage.

solutions.title: Redact secrets from CI logs
solutions.command_template:
# GitHub Actions — mask a value so it appears as *** in logs:
- name: Mask API key
  run: echo "::add-mask::${{ secrets.API_KEY }}"

# Never echo secrets directly:
# BAD:  echo "API_KEY=$API_KEY"
# GOOD: Use the secret only where needed, never print it
solutions.explanation: CI secrets must be masked so they never appear in job output. In GitHub Actions, secrets referenced via `${{ secrets.* }}` are automatically masked. Direct bash `echo` of a variable bypasses masking.

solutions.title: Enable repository secret scanning
solutions.command_template:
# GitHub Advanced Security — enable in repository settings:
# Settings → Code security and analysis → Secret scanning → Enable

# Add .gitleaks.toml for custom patterns:
[allowlist]
  description = "Allowlisted patterns"
  regexes = ['''^EXAMPLE_.*''']
solutions.explanation: Enable GitHub Advanced Security Secret Scanning or integrate GitGuardian to automatically detect and alert on exposed secrets before they are merged. Pre-commit hooks with gitleaks provide a first line of defense.

detection.regex: sql.injection|SQLi|CWE-89|taint.sql|OWASP.*A03|Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command|sql injection.*HIGH|finding.*sql.*injection

solutions.title: Use parameterized queries / prepared statements
solutions.command_template:
# BAD — direct string interpolation:
query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}"

# GOOD — parameterized query (Python/psycopg2):
cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s", (user_id,))

# GOOD — SQLAlchemy ORM:
user = session.query(User).filter(User.id == user_id).first()

# GOOD — Node.js/pg:
const result = await client.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [userId])
solutions.explanation: SQL injection occurs when user input is concatenated directly into SQL queries. Always use parameterized queries or an ORM — never string formatting or concatenation with user-supplied data.

solutions.title: Add input validation and allowlisting
solutions.explanation: Parameterized queries handle values. For dynamic identifiers (table/column names), use an explicit allowlist — these cannot be parameterized in most database drivers.

detection.regex: command.injection|CWE-78|OS Command Injection|subprocess.*shell=True.*user|os\.system.*user|exec.*user_input|Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command

solutions.title: Use subprocess with argument list, never shell=True with user input
solutions.command_template:
# BAD — shell=True with user input = RCE:
subprocess.run(f"git clone {user_url}", shell=True)
os.system(f"ping {hostname}")

# GOOD — pass arguments as a list:
subprocess.run(["git", "clone", user_url], shell=False, check=True)

# GOOD — validate input before use:
import re
if not re.match(r'^[a-zA-Z0-9./_-]+$', user_url):
    raise ValueError('Invalid URL characters')
subprocess.run(["git", "clone", user_url])
solutions.explanation: Command injection occurs when user-controlled data reaches a shell interpreter. Always pass arguments as a list to subprocess (never shell=True with user input), and validate input against a strict allowlist.

solutions.title: Use language-native APIs instead of shell commands
solutions.command_template:
# Instead of: os.system(f"rm -rf {path}")
import shutil
shutil.rmtree(path)

# Instead of: os.system(f"cp {src} {dst}")
import shutil
shutil.copy2(src, dst)

# Instead of: subprocess.run(f"curl {url}", shell=True)
import httpx
response = httpx.get(url)
solutions.explanation: For common file, network, and process operations, prefer language-native libraries over shell commands. They are not susceptible to shell metacharacter injection.

solutions.title: Upgrade the affected package to a patched version
solutions.command_template:
# npm:
npm audit
npm audit fix
npm install <package>@<patched-version>

# Python:
pip-audit
pip install --upgrade <package>

# Go:
govulncheck ./...
go get <module>@<patched-version>

# Ruby:
bundle audit
bundle update <gem>
solutions.explanation: The fastest remediation is upgrading the vulnerable package. Check the CVE advisory for the minimum patched version, then update your lockfile and re-run the audit to confirm the vulnerability is resolved.

solutions.title: Check if the vulnerable code path is actually reachable
solutions.explanation: Some CVEs affect features your application doesn't use (e.g., a deserialization vulnerability in a module you only use for string parsing). Tools like Snyk Reachability or GitHub Dependabot can assess reachability. Document as accepted risk if truly unreachable.

solutions.title: Add Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency updates
solutions.command_template:
# .github/dependabot.yml:
version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: npm
    directory: /
    schedule:
      interval: weekly
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10
solutions.explanation: Automated dependency update tools (Dependabot, Renovate) create PRs for new versions including security patches. Enable them to stay ahead of vulnerabilities proactively.

solutions.title: Use a minimal base image (distroless or Alpine)
solutions.command_template:
# Switch from ubuntu:latest to a minimal base:
# Dockerfile:
FROM gcr.io/distroless/python3-debian12
# or:
FROM python:3.12-alpine
# or for Go/static binaries:
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12

# Scan the new image:
trivy image python:3.12-alpine
solutions.explanation: Most container CVEs are in OS packages of the base image. Distroless images contain only your app and its runtime — no shell, no package manager, massively reduced attack surface. Alpine images are ~5MB and have fewer packages than Debian/Ubuntu.

solutions.title: Update base image tag and rebuild
solutions.command_template:
# Use a specific digest for reproducibility:
docker pull python:3.12-slim
docker build --no-cache -t myapp:latest .

# Scan result:
trivy image myapp:latest --severity CRITICAL,HIGH
solutions.explanation: Base image tags like `:3.12-slim` point to the latest patch. Rebuild with `--no-cache` to pull the latest base image, which often includes OS-level security patches.

solutions.title: Add container scanning to the CI pipeline
solutions.command_template:
# GitHub Actions with Trivy:
- name: Scan image
  uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
  with:
    image-ref: myapp:${{ github.sha }}
    format: sarif
    output: trivy-results.sarif
    severity: CRITICAL,HIGH
    exit-code: 1  # fail CI on CRITICAL findings
solutions.explanation: Integrate Trivy, Grype, or Snyk Container into your CI pipeline to block image pushes with critical vulnerabilities. Upload SARIF results to GitHub Security tab for tracking.

solutions.title: Restrict ingress rules to specific CIDR ranges
solutions.command_template:
# BAD — open to the internet:
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "http" {
  type        = "ingress"
  from_port   = 80
  to_port     = 80
  protocol    = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]  # ← never for SSH, DB, admin
}

# GOOD — restrict SSH to known IPs:
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "ssh" {
  type        = "ingress"
  from_port   = 22
  to_port     = 22
  protocol    = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/8", "192.168.1.0/24"]  # VPN/office only
}

# GOOD — allow HTTP from load balancer SG only:
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "http_from_alb" {
  type                     = "ingress"
  from_port                = 8080
  to_port                  = 8080
  protocol                 = "tcp"
  source_security_group_id = aws_security_group.alb.id  # SG reference
}
solutions.explanation: Security groups with 0.0.0.0/0 ingress expose services to the public internet. Only load balancers and public-facing endpoints should accept traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 on ports 80/443. SSH (22), databases (3306, 5432, 27017), and admin interfaces must never be publicly open.

solutions.title: Add Checkov or tfsec to pre-commit and CI
solutions.command_template:
# Install:
pip install checkov
npm install -g tfsec

# Run:
checkov -d . --framework terraform --check CKV_AWS_24,CKV_AWS_25
tfsec . --minimum-severity HIGH

# GitHub Actions:
- uses: bridgecrewio/checkov-action@master
  with:
    directory: .
    framework: terraform
    soft_fail: false
solutions.explanation: Integrate IaC security scanning into CI to catch open security groups before they reach production. Checkov and tfsec both check for CIS benchmark violations including open ingress rules.

patterns.name: Secret Scanning Pre-receive Hook Blocked Push

solutions.title: Remove the secret from the commit(s) and rewrite history
solutions.command_template:
# Step 1 — find where the secret is:
git log --all -p | grep -n 'AKIA\|ghp_\|BEGIN PRIVATE'

# Step 2 — remove from the last commit (if not yet pushed):
git reset HEAD~1
# Fix the file, then:
git add . && git commit -m "fix: remove secret"

# Step 3 — if already in multiple commits, use git-filter-repo:
pip install git-filter-repo
git filter-repo --path .env --invert-paths --force

# Step 4 — force push (coordinate with team):
git push --force-with-lease
solutions.explanation: GitHub Push Protection or a pre-receive gitleaks hook detected a secret in your commits. You must rewrite the git history to remove it — simply deleting the file in a new commit is not sufficient, as the secret remains in history.

solutions.title: Rotate the accidentally-committed secret immediately
solutions.explanation: Even before fixing the commit, rotate the credential as a priority. Assume the secret was captured by automated scanners the moment the push attempt was made. Identify the service (AWS, GitHub, Stripe, etc.) and revoke/rotate the credential.

solutions.title: Add pre-commit hooks to prevent future leaks
solutions.command_template:
# Install pre-commit:
pip install pre-commit

# .pre-commit-config.yaml:
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks
    rev: v8.18.0
    hooks:
      - id: gitleaks

# Install the hooks:
pre-commit install
solutions.explanation: Pre-commit hooks run gitleaks (or detect-secrets) locally before each commit, catching secrets before they ever reach the remote. This is the earliest and cheapest place to catch them.
